content.text:
I'm working in the GAIN Agentic Investigation Challenge repo at
C:\Users\rcalv\Projects\Northwestern Project\gain-investigation (deadline 2026-07-15).

Read CLAUDE.md, LEDGER.md, and DECISIONS.md first. LEDGER.md/DECISIONS.md are
intentionally empty — this is the second attempt at a fresh full-corpus pass; the first
one just re-ran queries/sweep_2026.sql wholesale and, unsurprisingly, re-derived the same
top-line anomalies already sitting in the archive (a self-reported vanity filing at the
top of spend rankings; a foreign-owned semiconductor company's disclosed ownership) —
re-running the exact SQL that originally found those things isn't independent
generation, it's mechanical replay. Do NOT open anything under `archive/`.

Context: `db/lda_full.duckdb` (2022-2026, all three datasets) is the canonical DB, with
entity-resolver tables already built in (`entities`, `entity_aliases`,
`registrant_crosswalk`) — though note `entity_aliases` has a known duplicate-row bug
inflating resolved-entity dollar totals; verify against raw aggregates before trusting
any $ total that goes through it.

Task: generate genuinely new candidate leads — not by running `queries/sweep_2026.sql`.

1. `.venv/Scripts/python tests/smoke_test.py` first.
2. Explore the schema yourself and write YOUR OWN SQL. Use `queries/sweep_2026.sql`
   only as a reference for conventions (dedup keyed on filing_period not filing_type,
   citation format, the named-actor rule) — not as a source of hypotheses. If you find
   yourself running one of its existing blocks unmodified, stop: that's replay, not
   generation.
3. Deliberately look at dimensions the existing lens file doesn't cover — for example:
   rate-of-change/trend analysis per registrant or client over the 2022-2026 span (not
   just point-in-time totals, which is all the existing blocks do); unusual
   lobbyist-to-firm staffing patterns (one person appearing across many unrelated
   clients/registrants); mismatches between a client's registered issue code and its
   self-described business; press-release-to-lobbying correlation at the INDIVIDUAL
   MEMBER level rather than the bill level; anything else your own judgment flags as
   worth a look. data_manual.md's "Starting points" sections are a reference for what
   kinds of questions this data supports, not a checklist to run once each.
4. If your own exploration surfaces something that turns out to already be an obviously
   dominant, mechanically-inevitable outlier (the single biggest number in a simple
   ranking) — that's a signal you're still on the surface, not that you've found
   something. Dig past the top-of-list result to what's underneath it.
5. Named-actor rule applies to anything you log as a lead: specific actor, date, record
   ID, or it doesn't pass triage.

End of session: update LEDGER.md, run `ledger_lint.py`, log any human-judgment moments in
DECISIONS.md, export the trace, commit and push.

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# GAIN Agentic Investigation Challenge — workspace guide
2	
3	Submission workspace for Northwestern's GAIN Agentic Investigation Challenge: an agentic
4	investigation of congressional lobbying filings (Senate + House LDA) and congressional press
5	releases, 2022–2026 Q1, asking who is paying whom to shape policy. **Deadline: 2026-07-15.**
6	README.md is the submission map (skills → findings → traces); this file is the working guide.
7	
8	## Layout
9	
10	```
11	gain-investigation/
12	├── README.md            ← submission map (six required elements; fill in as work happens)
13	├── CLAUDE.md            ← this file
14	├── LEDGER.md            ← investigation state: leads / entities checked / queries run / cold threads
15	├── DECISIONS.md         ← human-judgment log (required by the traces deliverable)
16	├── archive/             ← reset snapshots of LEDGER.md/DECISIONS.md (see note below); not submitted
17	├── skills/              ← the submitted Agent Skills, each self-contained (SKILL.md + scripts/)
18	│   ├── lda-corpus-loader/     build_db.py (raw corpus → DuckDB) + show_record.py (citation → raw record)
19	│   ├── lda-entity-resolver/   cross-dataset entity table (Senate↔House crosswalk)
20	│   ├── investigation-ledger/  ledger templates + ledger_lint.py
21	│   ├── lead-scanner/          lens SQL library + scanning discipline
22	│   ├── finding-verifier/      pre-lock claim re-derivation protocol
23	│   └── outside-context-scan/  exploratory novelty/news-landscape research (not verification)
24	├── queries/             ← lens SQL (sweep_2026.sql + run_sweep.py); cited by aggregate claims
25	├── findings/            ← one locked finding per file (locked = verification passed)
26	├── traces/              ← session JSONL exports + INDEX.md + rendered/ HTML
27	├── tests/               ← smoke_test.py + fixtures/ (tiny excerpts of real public records)
28	├── data/                ← raw corpus (gitignored; layout per the challenge data manual)
29	└── db/                  ← built DuckDB files (gitignored; rebuilt from raw via loader)
30	```
31	
32	## Commands (Windows dev box: `.venv/Scripts/python`; POSIX: `.venv/bin/python`)
33	
34	```bash
35	python -m venv .venv && .venv/Scripts/python -m pip install -r requirements.txt
36	.venv/Scripts/python tests/smoke_test.py                                  # green before anything else
37	.venv/Scripts/python skills/lda-corpus-loader/scripts/build_db.py \
38	    --data-root data/ --db db/lda.duckdb [--years 2025 2026] [--sample 2025-Q1]
39	.venv/Scripts/python skills/lda-corpus-loader/scripts/show_record.py <citation-key>
40	.venv/Scripts/python queries/run_sweep.py db/lda_full.duckdb [BLOCK-PREFIX]
41	.venv/Scripts/python skills/investigation-ledger/scripts/ledger_lint.py LEDGER.md
42	```
43	
44	Citation keys: Senate `filing_uuid` · House numeric XML filename (e.g. `301817772`) ·
45	press `src_file:src_line` (e.g. `congress_press/2026-01.jsonl:12`) — all stable identifiers
46	from the source systems, valid regardless of which DB below they were queried through.
47	
48	**Corpus versions** (all built the same way, differing only by `--years`; see
49	`skills/lda-corpus-loader/scripts/build_db.py`):
50	- `db/lda_full.duckdb` — 2022–2026, all years. **Canonical/primary as of 2026-07-06.**
51	  Start new investigative work here.
52	- `db/lda_pilot.duckdb` — 2025 + 2026-Q1 only. Kept solely to reproduce
53	  `findings/L010_pipe_materials_war.md`'s citations exactly as verified; not for new work.
54	- `db/lda_2026.duckdb` — 2026-Q1 only. Superseded by both of the above; safe to delete
55	  locally, nothing cites it that the other two can't also resolve.
56	
57	## Load-bearing conventions
58	
59	- **Never cite the database.** Record-level claims cite citation keys resolvable via
60	  `show_record.py`. Aggregate claims (spikes, rankings, counts) cite three things: the exact SQL
61	  (a labeled block in `queries/`), the one-command DB rebuild, and ≥3 sampled underlying records.
62	- **Raw corpus access goes through `show_record.py` only.** Never grep or open raw corpus files
63	  directly — 409K XMLs will eat your context and your citations won't be reproducible.
64	- **Every DB row carries a raw-record pointer** (`src_file` + `src_line`/`src_index`, or XML
65	  path). If you add a table, preserve this invariant.
66	- **Ledger discipline (single-writer).** Read `LEDGER.md` at session start; commit updates at
67	  session end and on any status change. Sub-agents return candidate rows; only the orchestrating
68	  session writes. Statuses: `open → triaged → investigating → verified | dead`, plus `parked`
69	  (cold, with revisit trigger). Run `ledger_lint.py` after edits.
70	- **Named-actor rule.** A lead with no specific actor, date, and record ID cannot pass triage.
71	  "Spend on issue X spiked" is a dataset summary; name who, when, and which filings.
72	- **DECISIONS.md** gets a row for every human intervention (triage picks, kills, editorial and
73	  legal-flag calls) with the trace file it happened in.
74	- **Traces.** Every working session is exported to `traces/YYYY-MM-DD_<skill-or-phase>_<lead>.jsonl`
75	  (session JSONL lives under `~/.claude/projects/<project-slug>/`) and indexed in `traces/INDEX.md`.
76	- **Findings lock only after verification.** A fresh session (no drafting context) re-derives
77	  every claim from cited records per `skills/finding-verifier/SKILL.md`, then the lock is logged
78	  in `DECISIONS.md`. The findings report is assembled from locked findings only.
79	- **Model/budget tiers.** Extraction and filtering stay in Python/SQL. Cheap models scan and rank
80	  (returning record IDs + one-line hypotheses, never quoted record text). Frontier models
81	  deep-read lead-attached records (cap ~30 records/session) and verify findings.
82	- **Exploratory outside-data checks (novelty/news-landscape) follow a fixed protocol,
83	  separate from finding-verifier's rigor** — anchor the search window to the actual event
84	  date (not "today"), run novelty and landscape checks as separate bounded passes (~3
85	  queries each), distill to a conclusion before it reaches the ledger. Full protocol in
86	  `skills/outside-context-scan/SKILL.md`. Established 2026-07-06 after searching
87	  "GlobalFoundries news 2026" for a 2024 event; deliberately kept out of
88	  `skills/finding-verifier/SKILL.md` since "verifier" implies a rigor this exploratory
89	  research doesn't have and shouldn't claim.
90	- **Self-contained language.** Submitted artifacts (SKILL.md files, findings, README) must stand
91	  alone — no references to internal planning docs or prior processes.
92	
93	## Data facts that bite (verified against real records 2026-07-04)
94	
95	- House quarterly XML uses `<alis><ali_info>` with `<issueAreaCode>` — NOT the `ali_Code` flat
96	  list the data manual describes (that may still apply to LD-1 registrations; unverified).
97	- Agencies lobbied are one comma-separated `<federal_agencies>` string per ali_info; splitting
98	  it is entity-resolver work.
99	- House forms pad with empty `<lobbyist>` slots — skip rows with no first and no last name.
100	- Senate registrant objects carry `house_registrant_id` — the clean Senate↔House crosswalk key.
101	- Senate LD-203 contributions are semiannual; early-year files are legitimately tiny.
102	- **Never sum filings without deduping — and the dedup key must be the period-invariant
103	  field, not the type code, applied identically on both chambers.** Registrants file
104	  duplicates (identical Senate Q1s posted 22 seconds apart) and amendments (Senate
105	  `filing_type` codes like `1A`/`2A`/`3A`/`4A`; House refilings under new filing_ids).
106	  Filtering senate to `filing_type LIKE 'Q%'` SILENTLY DROPS amendments — this bit twice:
107	  2026-07-04's un-deduped version fabricated a "House reports 2× Senate" pattern, and
108	  2026-07-06's H1c/H1d v1 (which filtered/partitioned by `filing_type`) fabricated a
109	  "chronic 10x cross-chamber mis-reporter" pattern that was actually senate's
110	  PRE-amendment figure vs house's POST-amendment figure (house wasn't affected because its
111	  directory-based period bucketing already folds originals+amendments together). Caught
112	  by a human cross-checking the live House portal, not by any internal check — a reminder
113	  that "the query ran and produced clean-looking numbers" is not verification. The correct
114	  pattern: dedup keyed on `filing_period` (constant across original+amendment, e.g.
115	  "second_quarter" for both `Q2` and `2A`), NOT `filing_type`, picking latest by `posted`/
116	  `filing_id` — see `queries/sweep_2026.sql#H1c` (current, fixed version) for the pattern.
117	- **Never sum senate + house datasets.** LD-2 quarterlies are filed with both chambers, so the
118	  two datasets are largely copies of the same filings. Dollar attribution is senate-primary
119	  (richer metadata); use house only to reconcile or fill gaps. Verified 2026-07-05 — the
120	  cross-dataset version (sweep#C1b) inflated per-bill totals ~40%.
121	- **House XML dumps are partial snapshots.** House 2026-Q1 holds 12,656 filings vs 21,145
122	  senate Q1s (the deadline-week flood is missing). Senate is the completeness reference for
123	  recent quarters; a filing absent house-side is expected noise, not a story.
124	- **Press and filings name bills differently.** Members write "the Farm Bill" / "NDAA"; filings
125	  cite H.R./S. numbers. Number-only matching fabricates "lobbied but publicly silent" bills
126	  (killed L004 that way). Say-vs-pay comparisons need alias matching or provision-level framing.
127	- **The Senate↔House join key is house `<senateID>` = `"<senate_registrant_id>-<senate_client_id>"`**
128	  (compound, engagement-level; verified 2026-07-06). Do NOT join senate `house_registrant_id`
129	  to house `<houseID>` — formats don't overlap (zero matches). Use `registrant_crosswalk`
130	  (entity-resolver) or the split_part pattern in `queries/sweep_2026.sql#H1c`.
131	- **`<houseID>` is NOT a resolvable filing_id/filename, and can collide with an unrelated
132	  one.** Verified 2026-07-06: a Mercury/PRVO Plinarsko Društvo quarterly filing carries
133	  `<houseID>301740622</houseID>`, but filing `301740622.xml` in this corpus is a completely
134	  different, unrelated registration (Cornerstone Government Affairs / National Association
135	  of Home Builders). `<houseID>` appears to be a persistent House-Clerk identifier for the
136	  registrant-client relationship (what the House disclosure portal's own search surfaces) —
137	  a different ID namespace from `filing_id` (the numeric filename), even though both are
138	  9-digit numbers that can coincidentally match an unrelated document. Never cite a
139	  `<houseID>` value as a `show_record.py` key; only the filename (`filing_id`) is a valid
140	  citation key on the House side.
141	- **Senate `client_id` is registrant-scoped, not global** — Comcast alone has 10+ client ids.
142	  Group clients by resolved entity (`entities`/`entity_aliases`, norm-key based), never by
143	  client_id. Registrant ids ARE global.
144	- Everything self-reported: strip whitespace everywhere, expect missing income/expenses, and
145	  treat gaps as potentially reportable rather than as noise.
146	- Windows: scripts force UTF-8 stdout (press text has curly quotes; pipes default to cp1252).
147	
148	## Session discipline for Claude sessions in this repo
149	
150	1. Start: read this file, `LEDGER.md`, and the tail of `DECISIONS.md`. Don't re-derive state.
151	2. Work one lead or one phase per session where practical (keeps traces reviewable).
152	3. End: update `LEDGER.md` (+ lint), log any human decisions, export the session trace, update
153	   `traces/INDEX.md`, commit.
154	
155	Internal planning context (not part of the submission, not for citation in submitted artifacts):
156	`../_Plan.md` in the parent folder holds the phased plan and decision register.
157	
158	**2026-07-06 reset:** `LEDGER.md`/`DECISIONS.md` were emptied and the prior pilot-scale content
159	moved to `archive/*_pilot-triage_2026-07-06.md`, so a session working the newly-built full corpus
160	(`db/lda_full.duckdb`) starts with a clean, unbiased view rather than inheriting pilot-era lead
161	framing. This was a deliberate one-time QA reset, not a standing practice — don't repeat it on a
162	future corpus rebuild without being asked. The archived files are historical reference only; do
163	not read them into context unless specifically asked to reconcile or restore something from them
164	(e.g. `findings/L010_pipe_materials_war.md` is untouched and still a real, independently-verified
165	finding regardless of this reset — it just isn't re-listed as a lead in the fresh ledger).
166

# GAIN Agentic Investigation Challenge — workspace guide

Submission workspace for Northwestern's GAIN Agentic Investigation Challenge: an agentic
investigation of congressional lobbying filings (Senate + House LDA) and congressional press
releases, 2022–2026 Q1, asking who is paying whom to shape policy. **Deadline: 2026-07-15.**
README.md is the submission map (skills → findings → traces); this file is the working guide.

## Layout

```
gain-investigation/
├── README.md            ← submission map (six required elements; fill in as work happens)
├── CLAUDE.md            ← this file
├── LEDGER.md            ← investigation state: leads / entities checked / queries run / cold threads
├── DECISIONS.md         ← human-judgment log (required by the traces deliverable)
├── archive/             ← reset snapshots of LEDGER.md/DECISIONS.md (see note below); not submitted
├── skills/              ← the submitted Agent Skills, each self-contained (SKILL.md + scripts/)
│   ├── lda-corpus-loader/     build_db.py (raw corpus → DuckDB) + show_record.py (citation → raw record)
│   ├── lda-entity-resolver/   cross-dataset entity table (Senate↔House crosswalk)
│   ├── investigation-ledger/  ledger templates + ledger_lint.py
│   ├── lead-scanner/          lens SQL library + scanning discipline
│   ├── finding-verifier/      pre-lock claim re-derivation protocol
│   └── outside-context-scan/  exploratory novelty/news-landscape research (not verification)
├── queries/             ← lens SQL (sweep_2026.sql + run_sweep.py); cited by aggregate claims
├── findings/            ← one locked finding per file (locked = verification passed)
├── traces/              ← session JSONL exports + INDEX.md + rendered/ HTML
├── tests/               ← smoke_test.py + fixtures/ (tiny excerpts of real public records)
├── data/                ← raw corpus (gitignored; layout per the challenge data manual)
└── db/                  ← built DuckDB files (gitignored; rebuilt from raw via loader)
```

## Commands (Windows dev box: `.venv/Scripts/python`; POSIX: `.venv/bin/python`)

```bash
python -m venv .venv && .venv/Scripts/python -m pip install -r requirements.txt
.venv/Scripts/python tests/smoke_test.py                                  # green before anything else
.venv/Scripts/python skills/lda-corpus-loader/scripts/build_db.py \
    --data-root data/ --db db/lda.duckdb [--years 2025 2026] [--sample 2025-Q1]
.venv/Scripts/python skills/lda-corpus-loader/scripts/show_record.py <citation-key>
.venv/Scripts/python queries/run_sweep.py db/lda_full.duckdb [BLOCK-PREFIX]
.venv/Scripts/python skills/investigation-ledger/scripts/ledger_lint.py LEDGER.md
```

Citation keys: Senate `filing_uuid` · House numeric XML filename (e.g. `301817772`) ·
press `src_file:src_line` (e.g. `congress_press/2026-01.jsonl:12`) — all stable identifiers
from the source systems, valid regardless of which DB below they were queried through.

**Corpus versions** (all built the same way, differing only by `--years`; see
`skills/lda-corpus-loader/scripts/build_db.py`):
- `db/lda_full.duckdb` — 2022–2026, all years. **Canonical/primary as of 2026-07-06.**
  Start new investigative work here.
- `db/lda_pilot.duckdb` — 2025 + 2026-Q1 only. Kept solely to reproduce
  `findings/L010_pipe_materials_war.md`'s citations exactly as verified; not for new work.
- `db/lda_2026.duckdb` — 2026-Q1 only. Superseded by both of the above; safe to delete
  locally, nothing cites it that the other two can't also resolve.

## Load-bearing conventions

- **Never cite the database.** Record-level claims cite citation keys resolvable via
  `show_record.py`. Aggregate claims (spikes, rankings, counts) cite three things: the exact SQL
  (a labeled block in `queries/`), the one-command DB rebuild, and ≥3 sampled underlying records.
- **Raw corpus access goes through `show_record.py` only.** Never grep or open raw corpus files
  directly — 409K XMLs will eat your context and your citations won't be reproducible.
- **Every DB row carries a raw-record pointer** (`src_file` + `src_line`/`src_index`, or XML
  path). If you add a table, preserve this invariant.
- **Ledger discipline (single-writer).** Read `LEDGER.md` at session start; commit updates at
  session end and on any status change. Sub-agents return candidate rows; only the orchestrating
  session writes. Statuses: `open → triaged → investigating → verified | dead`, plus `parked`
  (cold, with revisit trigger). Run `ledger_lint.py` after edits.
- **Named-actor rule.** A lead with no specific actor, date, and record ID cannot pass triage.
  "Spend on issue X spiked" is a dataset summary; name who, when, and which filings.
- **DECISIONS.md** gets a row for every human intervention (triage picks, kills, editorial and
  legal-flag calls) with the trace file it happened in.
- **Traces.** Every working session is exported to `traces/YYYY-MM-DD_<skill-or-phase>_<lead>.jsonl`
  (session JSONL lives under `~/.claude/projects/<project-slug>/`) and indexed in `traces/INDEX.md`.
- **Findings lock only after verification.** A fresh session (no drafting context) re-derives
  every claim from cited records per `skills/finding-verifier/SKILL.md`, then the lock is logged
  in `DECISIONS.md`. The findings report is assembled from locked findings only.
- **Model/budget tiers.** Extraction and filtering stay in Python/SQL. Cheap models scan and rank
  (returning record IDs + one-line hypotheses, never quoted record text). Frontier models
  deep-read lead-attached records (cap ~30 records/session) and verify findings.
- **Exploratory outside-data checks (novelty/news-landscape) follow a fixed protocol,
  separate from finding-verifier's rigor** — anchor the search window to the actual event
  date (not "today"), run novelty and landscape checks as separate bounded passes (~3
  queries each), distill to a conclusion before it reaches the ledger. Full protocol in
  `skills/outside-context-scan/SKILL.md`. Established 2026-07-06 after searching
  "GlobalFoundries news 2026" for a 2024 event; deliberately kept out of
  `skills/finding-verifier/SKILL.md` since "verifier" implies a rigor this exploratory
  research doesn't have and shouldn't claim.
- **Self-contained language.** Submitted artifacts (SKILL.md files, findings, README) must stand
  alone — no references to internal planning docs or prior processes.

## Data facts that bite (verified against real records 2026-07-04)

- House quarterly XML uses `<alis><ali_info>` with `<issueAreaCode>` — NOT the `ali_Code` flat
  list the data manual describes (that may still apply to LD-1 registrations; unverified).
- Agencies lobbied are one comma-separated `<federal_agencies>` string per ali_info; splitting
  it is entity-resolver work.
- House forms pad with empty `<lobbyist>` slots — skip rows with no first and no last name.
- Senate registrant objects carry `house_registrant_id` — the clean Senate↔House crosswalk key.
- Senate LD-203 contributions are semiannual; early-year files are legitimately tiny.
- **Never sum filings without deduping — and the dedup key must be the period-invariant
  field, not the type code, applied identically on both chambers.** Registrants file
  duplicates (identical Senate Q1s posted 22 seconds apart) and amendments (Senate
  `filing_type` codes like `1A`/`2A`/`3A`/`4A`; House refilings under new filing_ids).
  Filtering senate to `filing_type LIKE 'Q%'` SILENTLY DROPS amendments — this bit twice:
  2026-07-04's un-deduped version fabricated a "House reports 2× Senate" pattern, and
  2026-07-06's H1c/H1d v1 (which filtered/partitioned by `filing_type`) fabricated a
  "chronic 10x cross-chamber mis-reporter" pattern that was actually senate's
  PRE-amendment figure vs house's POST-amendment figure (house wasn't affected because its
  directory-based period bucketing already folds originals+amendments together). Caught
  by a human cross-checking the live House portal, not by any internal check — a reminder
  that "the query ran and produced clean-looking numbers" is not verification. The correct
  pattern: dedup keyed on `filing_period` (constant across original+amendment, e.g.
  "second_quarter" for both `Q2` and `2A`), NOT `filing_type`, picking latest by `posted`/
  `filing_id` — see `queries/sweep_2026.sql#H1c` (current, fixed version) for the pattern.
- **Never sum senate + house datasets.** LD-2 quarterlies are filed with both chambers, so the
  two datasets are largely copies of the same filings. Dollar attribution is senate-primary
  (richer metadata); use house only to reconcile or fill gaps. Verified 2026-07-05 — the
  cross-dataset version (sweep#C1b) inflated per-bill totals ~40%.
- **House XML dumps are partial snapshots.** House 2026-Q1 holds 12,656 filings vs 21,145
  senate Q1s (the deadline-week flood is missing). Senate is the completeness reference for
  recent quarters; a filing absent house-side is expected noise, not a story.
- **Press and filings name bills differently.** Members write "the Farm Bill" / "NDAA"; filings
  cite H.R./S. numbers. Number-only matching fabricates "lobbied but publicly silent" bills
  (killed L004 that way). Say-vs-pay comparisons need alias matching or provision-level framing.
- **The Senate↔House join key is house `<senateID>` = `"<senate_registrant_id>-<senate_client_id>"`**
  (compound, engagement-level; verified 2026-07-06). Do NOT join senate `house_registrant_id`
  to house `<houseID>` — formats don't overlap (zero matches). Use `registrant_crosswalk`
  (entity-resolver) or the split_part pattern in `queries/sweep_2026.sql#H1c`.
- **`<houseID>` is NOT a resolvable filing_id/filename, and can collide with an unrelated
  one.** Verified 2026-07-06: a Mercury/PRVO Plinarsko Društvo quarterly filing carries
  `<houseID>301740622</houseID>`, but filing `301740622.xml` in this corpus is a completely
  different, unrelated registration (Cornerstone Government Affairs / National Association
  of Home Builders). `<houseID>` appears to be a persistent House-Clerk identifier for the
  registrant-client relationship (what the House disclosure portal's own search surfaces) —
  a different ID namespace from `filing_id` (the numeric filename), even though both are
  9-digit numbers that can coincidentally match an unrelated document. Never cite a
  `<houseID>` value as a `show_record.py` key; only the filename (`filing_id`) is a valid
  citation key on the House side.
- **Senate `client_id` is registrant-scoped, not global** — Comcast alone has 10+ client ids.
  Group clients by resolved entity (`entities`/`entity_aliases`, norm-key based), never by
  client_id. Registrant ids ARE global.
- Everything self-reported: strip whitespace everywhere, expect missing income/expenses, and
  treat gaps as potentially reportable rather than as noise.
- Windows: scripts force UTF-8 stdout (press text has curly quotes; pipes default to cp1252).

## Session discipline for Claude sessions in this repo

1. Start: read this file, `LEDGER.md`, and the tail of `DECISIONS.md`. Don't re-derive state.
2. Work one lead or one phase per session where practical (keeps traces reviewable).
3. End: update `LEDGER.md` (+ lint), log any human decisions, export the session trace, update
   `traces/INDEX.md`, commit.

Internal planning context (not part of the submission, not for citation in submitted artifacts):
`../_Plan.md` in the parent folder holds the phased plan and decision register.

**2026-07-06 reset:** `LEDGER.md`/`DECISIONS.md` were emptied and the prior pilot-scale content
moved to `archive/*_pilot-triage_2026-07-06.md`, so a session working the newly-built full corpus
(`db/lda_full.duckdb`) starts with a clean, unbiased view rather than inheriting pilot-era lead
framing. This was a deliberate one-time QA reset, not a standing practice — don't repeat it on a
future corpus rebuild without being asked. The archived files are historical reference only; do
not read them into context unless specifically asked to reconcile or restore something from them
(e.g. `findings/L010_pipe_materials_war.md` is untouched and still a real, independently-verified
finding regardless of this reset — it just isn't re-listed as a lead in the fresh ledger).

file.content:
# Investigation Ledger

Single source of truth for what has been checked, what is open, which entities matter, and which
threads went cold. **Write protocol:** sub-agents never edit this file — they return candidate rows;
only the orchestrating session (or a human) commits them. Read at session start; write at any status
change and at session end.

**Status state machine:** `open → triaged → investigating → verified | dead`. Any lead may move to
`parked` (cold) with a reason and a revisit trigger. **Cold ≠ dead:** dead = refuted or judged not
newsworthy (reason recorded); cold = promising but blocked/deprioritized, reconsidered at every
triage checkpoint. New leads arriving mid-stream enter as `open` and are considered at the next
triage checkpoint.

**Named-actor rule:** a lead with no specific actor, date, and record ID cannot pass triage.

**Fresh start (2026-07-06, second attempt):** this ledger was reset a second time. The first
full-corpus attempt ran `queries/sweep_2026.sql` wholesale and, unsurprisingly, re-derived the same
top-line anomalies already documented in the pilot archive (a self-reported vanity filing at the top
of spend rankings; a foreign-owned semiconductor company's disclosed ownership) — re-running the
exact SQL that originally found those things just re-finds them, it isn't independent generation.
That attempt is archived at `archive/LEDGER_full-sweep-rediscovery_2026-07-06.md`. The prior pilot
pass is separately archived at `archive/LEDGER_pilot-triage_2026-07-06.md`. This ledger starts empty
again for a session designing its own exploratory queries rather than replaying `sweep_2026.sql`.

## Leads

| id | hypothesis (one line) | lens | named actors | status | owner | evidence so far (record IDs) | next action | updated |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|

## Entities checked

| entity (entity_id) | verdict | records examined | date |
|---|---|---|---|

## Queries run

| date | SQL (file in queries/) | one-line result |
|---|---|---|

## Cold threads

| lead id | parked reason | revisit trigger | date parked |
|---|---|---|---|

content.content:
1	# Data Manual
2	
3	This dataset is stored under `data/`. Together the files cover congressional communications and the lobbying activity aimed at Congress from 2022 through Q1 2026. This manual describes the current on-disk layout, how to load the data, and where to start digging.
4	
5	## Data layout
6	
7	The `data/` directory is decompressed.
8	
9	```
10	data/
11	  congress_press/
12	    2022/2022-01.jsonl ... 2022-12.jsonl
13	    2023/2023-01.jsonl ... 2023-12.jsonl
14	    2024/2024-01.jsonl ... 2024-12.jsonl
15	    2025/2025-01.jsonl ... 2025-12.jsonl
16	    2026-01.jsonl
17	    2026-02.jsonl
18	    2026-03.jsonl
19	  senate/
20	    2022/ 2023/ 2024/ 2025/ 2026/
21	    constants/
22	  house/
23	    2022_Registrations_XML/
24	    2022_1stQuarter_XML/ ... 2022_4thQuarter_XML/
25	    2023_Registrations_XML/ ... 2023_4thQuarter_XML/
26	    2024_Registrations_XML/ ... 2024_4thQuarter_XML/
27	    2025_Registrations_XML/ ... 2025_4thQuarter_XML/
28	    2026_Registrations_XML/
29	    2026_1stQuarter_XML/
30	```
31	
32	## At a glance
33	
34	| Dataset | Path | Format | Coverage | On-disk size |
35	|---|---|---|---|---|
36	| Congress press releases | `data/congress_press/` | JSONL (51 decompressed files) | 2022 – 2026-03 | 504 MB |
37	| Senate LDA filings & contributions | `data/senate/` | JSON (arrays, nested) | 2022 – 2026 Q1 | 2.2 GB |
38	| House LDA registrations & reports | `data/house/` | XML (409,650 decompressed files) | 2022 – 2026 Q1 | 5.9 GB |
39	
40	All three are public records. Expect self-reported data, missing fields, and inconsistent conventions — part of the investigative work is reconciling them.
41	
42	---
43	
44	## 1. Congress press releases — `data/congress_press/`
45	
46	**Description.** Press releases scraped from the official `*.house.gov` and `*.senate.gov` member websites. Each record is one release with light member metadata and the full body text.
47	
48	**Scale.** ~48K releases in 2025 alone (≈478 members represented); the full 2022–2026-Q1 corpus runs larger. Average release ~900 words → tens of millions of words of unstructured text.
49	
50	**Format.** Newline-delimited JSON (JSONL). One record per line. The full years 2022 through 2025 are split into year directories; the 2026 Q1 files sit directly in `data/congress_press/`.
51	
52	```
53	data/congress_press/
54	  2022/
55	    2022-01.jsonl ... 2022-12.jsonl
56	  2023/
57	    2023-01.jsonl ... 2023-12.jsonl
58	  2024/
59	    2024-01.jsonl ... 2024-12.jsonl
60	  2025/
61	    2025-01.jsonl ... 2025-12.jsonl
62	  2026-01.jsonl
63	  2026-02.jsonl
64	  2026-03.jsonl
65	```
66	
67	**Record fields.** `url`, `title`, `date`, `date_source`, `source` (member press-release index page), `domain`, `scraper` (scraper ID), `member` (`bioguide_id`, `name`, `party`, `state`, `chamber`), `text` (full body, newline-preserved).
68	
69	**Source.** [Congress Press](https://thescoop.org/congress-press/)
70	
71	**Timeframe.** 2022-01 through 2026-03. The 2026 files currently include January through March at the root of `data/congress_press/`.
72	
73	**Load it.**
74	
75	```python
76	import json
77	from pathlib import Path
78	
79	root = Path("data/congress_press")
80	
81	# Single month
82	with open(root / "2026-01.jsonl") as f:
83	    for line in f:
84	        rec = json.loads(line)
85	        print(rec["member"]["name"], rec["title"])
86	
87	# Full decompressed year
88	for path in sorted((root / "2025").glob("*.jsonl")):
89	    with open(path) as f:
90	        for line in f:
91	            rec = json.loads(line)
92	            # ...
93	```
94	
95	**Starting points.**
96	- Keyword and frequency analysis: who talks about a given issue, how often, and when does the language shift.
97	- Comparative messaging: same event, different party framing — pull all releases from a +/- 7-day window around a news event and cluster.
98	- Member activity baselines: releases/month per member; outliers often correlate with primary campaigns, committee news, or scandal cycles.
99	- Entity extraction: NER over `text` to pull companies, agencies, and bills — the output bridges naturally to the lobbying datasets via `bioguide_id` and entity names.
100	
101	---
102	
103	## 2. Senate LDA data — `data/senate/`
104	
105	**Description.** Lobbying Disclosure Act filings submitted to the Secretary of the Senate: registrations, quarterly activity reports, and semiannual contribution (LD-203) reports. This is the "who is lobbying whom, on what, for how much" dataset.
106	
107	**Scale (2025).** 108,225 filings, 39,438 contribution reports (with ~149K line items across them), 5,470 registrants, 28,246 clients. Earlier years are comparable.
108	
109	**Format.** JSON arrays, deeply nested. Each filing includes its registrant, client, lobbying activities (with ALI issue codes and lobbyists), and government entities lobbied. Contribution reports include a `pacs` and `contribution_items` list.
110	
111	```
112	data/senate/
113	  2022/ 2023/ 2024/ 2025/ 2026/
114	    filings/filings_{year}.json          # array of filing objects
115	    contributions/contributions_{year}.json
116	  constants/
117	    countries.json
118	    filing_types.json                    # RR, Q1..Q4, MM (mid-year), YE (year-end), etc.
119	    government_entities.json             # agencies/chambers lobbied
120	    lobbying_activity_issues.json        # 3-letter ALI codes (BUD, TAX, HCR, …)
121	    states.json
122	    lobbyist_prefixes.json
123	    lobbyist_suffixes.json
124	    contribution_item_types.json
125	```
126	
127	**Key fields.** Filing: `filing_uuid`, `filing_type`, `filing_period`, `filing_year`, `income`, `expenses`, `registrant` (nested), `client` (nested), `lobbying_activities[]` (each with `general_issue_code`, `description`, `lobbyists[]`, `government_entities[]`). Contributions: `filing_uuid`, `filer_type`, `registrant`, `lobbyist`, `pacs[]`, `contribution_items[]` (with `type`, `amount`, `payee`, `honoree`, `contributor_name`).
128	
129	**Source.** [Senate LDA API](https://lda.senate.gov/api/v1/) (`filings`, `contributions` endpoints).
130	
131	**Timeframe.** 2022-01-01 through 2026-03-31 (2026 is Q1 only).
132	
133	**Load it.**
134	
135	```python
136	import json
137	
138	with open("data/senate/2025/filings/filings_2025.json") as f:
139	    filings = json.load(f)           # list of dicts
140	
141	print(len(filings), "filings")
142	f0 = filings[0]
143	print(f0["registrant"]["name"], "→", f0["client"]["name"])
144	for act in f0["lobbying_activities"]:
145	    print(" ", act["general_issue_code"], act["description"][:80])
146	
147	# Decode issue codes using the constants file
148	issues = {c["value"]: c["name"]
149	          for c in json.load(open("data/senate/constants/lobbying_activity_issues.json"))}
150	```
151	
152	**Starting points.**
153	- Top-of-funnel aggregates: biggest spenders by client, most-active registrants, busiest issue codes per quarter.
154	- Revolving door: the `covered_position` field on each lobbyist flags prior government roles — mine it for text patterns ("Chief of Staff to Sen. …").
155	- Foreign influence: filter on `foreign_entities[]` across filings; cross-reference with FARA.
156	- Contribution flows: `contribution_items[].payee` vs. `honoree` reveals who lobbyists and their PACs are funding. Join to FEC for validation.
157	- Data-quality stories: Many filings lack income data, expenses, and state info. The gaps are themselves reportable.
158	
159	---
160	
161	## 3. House LDA data — `data/house/`
162	
163	**Description.** The House Clerk's parallel set of LDA disclosures. Registrations (LD-1) and quarterly activity reports (LD-2). Same underlying disclosure regime as the Senate data, different distribution format and slightly different schema.
164	
165	**Scale (2025).** ~7,762 registrations + ~100,760 quarterly records ≈ 108K records, comparable to the Senate side. The full extracted House tree contains 409,650 XML files.
166	
167	**Format.** One XML document per filing. Each reporting period is stored in its own directory.
168	
169	```
170	data/house/
171	  2022_Registrations_XML/
172	    *.xml
173	  2022_1stQuarter_XML/ ... 2022_4thQuarter_XML/
174	  2023_Registrations_XML/ ... 2023_4thQuarter_XML/
175	  2024_Registrations_XML/ ... 2024_4thQuarter_XML/
176	  2025_Registrations_XML/ ... 2025_4thQuarter_XML/
177	  2026_Registrations_XML/
178	  2026_1stQuarter_XML/
179	```
180	
181	Root element is `<LOBBYINGDISCLOSURE1>` (registrations) or `<LOBBYINGDISCLOSURE2>` (quarterlies). Filenames are numeric House filing IDs (e.g. `301642857.xml`).
182	
183	**Key fields.** `organizationName`, `clientName`, `senateID`, `houseID`, `lobbyists/lobbyist[]` (with `coveredPosition`, `lobbyistNew`), `alis/ali_Code[]` (ALI issue codes — up to 9 slots, often sparse), `specific_issues/description` (free text, often naming specific bills and agencies), `income`/`expenses`, `governmentEntities`, `foreignEntities`.
184	
185	**Source.** [House Clerk Lobbying Disclosure](https://disclosurespreview.house.gov/).
186	
187	**Timeframe.** 2022-01-01 through 2026-03-31 (2026 = Q1 + registrations).
188	
189	**Load it.**
190	
191	```python
192	from pathlib import Path
193	import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET
194	
195	path = Path("data/house/2025_1stQuarter_XML")
196	for xml_path in sorted(path.glob("*.xml"))[:5]:
197	    root = ET.parse(xml_path).getroot()
198	    org = root.findtext("organizationName", "").strip()
199	    client = root.findtext("clientName", "").strip()
200	    issues = [a.text for a in root.findall(".//alis/ali_Code") if (a.text or "").strip()]
201	    print(xml_path.name, "|", org, "→", client, "|", issues)
202	```
203	
204	Expect a fair amount of whitespace-only text inside elements; `.strip()` everything. Many quarterly filings carry the same `senateID` as a Senate filing — that's the bridge between the two lobbying datasets.
205	
206	**Starting points.**
207	- Senate↔House reconciliation: fuzzy-match on `organizationName`/`clientName` and validate with the shared `senateID`/`houseID`. Discrepancies between the two filings for the same engagement are newsworthy.
208	- Bill-level lobbying maps: `specific_issues/description` often names bill numbers (`H.R. 1234`) — extract and join against Congress.gov bill and vote data.
209	- Entity-resolution challenge: the House data has no UUIDs and no standardized casing; building a clean entity table across Senate + House is itself a deliverable.
210	
211	---
212	
213	## Cross-dataset leads
214	
215	The highest-value questions require more than one of these:
216	
217	- **"Say vs. pay":** For a member (keyed by `bioguide_id` in the press corpus), correlate press-release topics with lobbying activity targeting their chamber/committee around the same quarter.
218	- **Temporal lobbying–messaging coupling:** When quarterly lobbying spend on an issue spikes, does the language in press releases shift? Good test for causal-adjacent narrative work.
219	- **Entity graph:** Press-release NER → companies/orgs → Senate/House LDA registrants & clients → government entities lobbied → committees → members. The cross-walks are the investigation.
220	
221	## Useful external enrichments
222	
223	- [Congress.gov bulk data / API](https://www.congress.gov/help/using-data-offsite) — bills, votes, committee assignments, cosponsorships. Joins via `bioguide_id`.
224	- [FEC](https://www.fec.gov/data/) — campaign finance to ground-truth lobbyist contributions.
225	- [FARA](https://efile.fara.gov/ords/fara/r/fara_ws/api/bulkdata) — foreign-agent filings, complements the `foreign_entities` fields.
226	- [Federal Register](https://www.federalregister.gov/developers/api/v1) — regulatory outcomes of lobbying on specific rules.
227

file.content:
# Data Manual

This dataset is stored under `data/`. Together the files cover congressional communications and the lobbying activity aimed at Congress from 2022 through Q1 2026. This manual describes the current on-disk layout, how to load the data, and where to start digging.

## Data layout

The `data/` directory is decompressed.

```
data/
  congress_press/
    2022/2022-01.jsonl ... 2022-12.jsonl
    2023/2023-01.jsonl ... 2023-12.jsonl
    2024/2024-01.jsonl ... 2024-12.jsonl
    2025/2025-01.jsonl ... 2025-12.jsonl
    2026-01.jsonl
    2026-02.jsonl
    2026-03.jsonl
  senate/
    2022/ 2023/ 2024/ 2025/ 2026/
    constants/
  house/
    2022_Registrations_XML/
    2022_1stQuarter_XML/ ... 2022_4thQuarter_XML/
    2023_Registrations_XML/ ... 2023_4thQuarter_XML/
    2024_Registrations_XML/ ... 2024_4thQuarter_XML/
    2025_Registrations_XML/ ... 2025_4thQuarter_XML/
    2026_Registrations_XML/
    2026_1stQuarter_XML/
```

## At a glance

| Dataset | Path | Format | Coverage | On-disk size |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Congress press releases | `data/congress_press/` | JSONL (51 decompressed files) | 2022 – 2026-03 | 504 MB |
| Senate LDA filings & contributions | `data/senate/` | JSON (arrays, nested) | 2022 – 2026 Q1 | 2.2 GB |
| House LDA registrations & reports | `data/house/` | XML (409,650 decompressed files) | 2022 – 2026 Q1 | 5.9 GB |

All three are public records. Expect self-reported data, missing fields, and inconsistent conventions — part of the investigative work is reconciling them.

---

## 1. Congress press releases — `data/congress_press/`

**Description.** Press releases scraped from the official `*.house.gov` and `*.senate.gov` member websites. Each record is one release with light member metadata and the full body text.

**Scale.** ~48K releases in 2025 alone (≈478 members represented); the full 2022–2026-Q1 corpus runs larger. Average release ~900 words → tens of millions of words of unstructured text.

**Format.** Newline-delimited JSON (JSONL). One record per line. The full years 2022 through 2025 are split into year directories; the 2026 Q1 files sit directly in `data/congress_press/`.

```
data/congress_press/
  2022/
    2022-01.jsonl ... 2022-12.jsonl
  2023/
    2023-01.jsonl ... 2023-12.jsonl
  2024/
    2024-01.jsonl ... 2024-12.jsonl
  2025/
    2025-01.jsonl ... 2025-12.jsonl
  2026-01.jsonl
  2026-02.jsonl
  2026-03.jsonl
```

**Record fields.** `url`, `title`, `date`, `date_source`, `source` (member press-release index page), `domain`, `scraper` (scraper ID), `member` (`bioguide_id`, `name`, `party`, `state`, `chamber`), `text` (full body, newline-preserved).

**Source.** [Congress Press](https://thescoop.org/congress-press/)

**Timeframe.** 2022-01 through 2026-03. The 2026 files currently include January through March at the root of `data/congress_press/`.

**Load it.**

```python
import json
from pathlib import Path

root = Path("data/congress_press")

# Single month
with open(root / "2026-01.jsonl") as f:
    for line in f:
        rec = json.loads(line)
        print(rec["member"]["name"], rec["title"])

# Full decompressed year
for path in sorted((root / "2025").glob("*.jsonl")):
    with open(path) as f:
        for line in f:
            rec = json.loads(line)
            # ...
```

**Starting points.**
- Keyword and frequency analysis: who talks about a given issue, how often, and when does the language shift.
- Comparative messaging: same event, different party framing — pull all releases from a +/- 7-day window around a news event and cluster.
- Member activity baselines: releases/month per member; outliers often correlate with primary campaigns, committee news, or scandal cycles.
- Entity extraction: NER over `text` to pull companies, agencies, and bills — the output bridges naturally to the lobbying datasets via `bioguide_id` and entity names.

---

## 2. Senate LDA data — `data/senate/`

**Description.** Lobbying Disclosure Act filings submitted to the Secretary of the Senate: registrations, quarterly activity reports, and semiannual contribution (LD-203) reports. This is the "who is lobbying whom, on what, for how much" dataset.

**Scale (2025).** 108,225 filings, 39,438 contribution reports (with ~149K line items across them), 5,470 registrants, 28,246 clients. Earlier years are comparable.

**Format.** JSON arrays, deeply nested. Each filing includes its registrant, client, lobbying activities (with ALI issue codes and lobbyists), and government entities lobbied. Contribution reports include a `pacs` and `contribution_items` list.

```
data/senate/
  2022/ 2023/ 2024/ 2025/ 2026/
    filings/filings_{year}.json          # array of filing objects
    contributions/contributions_{year}.json
  constants/
    countries.json
    filing_types.json                    # RR, Q1..Q4, MM (mid-year), YE (year-end), etc.
    government_entities.json             # agencies/chambers lobbied
    lobbying_activity_issues.json        # 3-letter ALI codes (BUD, TAX, HCR, …)
    states.json
    lobbyist_prefixes.json
    lobbyist_suffixes.json
    contribution_item_types.json
```

**Key fields.** Filing: `filing_uuid`, `filing_type`, `filing_period`, `filing_year`, `income`, `expenses`, `registrant` (nested), `client` (nested), `lobbying_activities[]` (each with `general_issue_code`, `description`, `lobbyists[]`, `government_entities[]`). Contributions: `filing_uuid`, `filer_type`, `registrant`, `lobbyist`, `pacs[]`, `contribution_items[]` (with `type`, `amount`, `payee`, `honoree`, `contributor_name`).

**Source.** [Senate LDA API](https://lda.senate.gov/api/v1/) (`filings`, `contributions` endpoints).

**Timeframe.** 2022-01-01 through 2026-03-31 (2026 is Q1 only).

**Load it.**

```python
import json

with open("data/senate/2025/filings/filings_2025.json") as f:
    filings = json.load(f)           # list of dicts

print(len(filings), "filings")
f0 = filings[0]
print(f0["registrant"]["name"], "→", f0["client"]["name"])
for act in f0["lobbying_activities"]:
    print(" ", act["general_issue_code"], act["description"][:80])

# Decode issue codes using the constants file
issues = {c["value"]: c["name"]
          for c in json.load(open("data/senate/constants/lobbying_activity_issues.json"))}
```

**Starting points.**
- Top-of-funnel aggregates: biggest spenders by client, most-active registrants, busiest issue codes per quarter.
- Revolving door: the `covered_position` field on each lobbyist flags prior government roles — mine it for text patterns ("Chief of Staff to Sen. …").
- Foreign influence: filter on `foreign_entities[]` across filings; cross-reference with FARA.
- Contribution flows: `contribution_items[].payee` vs. `honoree` reveals who lobbyists and their PACs are funding. Join to FEC for validation.
- Data-quality stories: Many filings lack income data, expenses, and state info. The gaps are themselves reportable.

---

## 3. House LDA data — `data/house/`

**Description.** The House Clerk's parallel set of LDA disclosures. Registrations (LD-1) and quarterly activity reports (LD-2). Same underlying disclosure regime as the Senate data, different distribution format and slightly different schema.

**Scale (2025).** ~7,762 registrations + ~100,760 quarterly records ≈ 108K records, comparable to the Senate side. The full extracted House tree contains 409,650 XML files.

**Format.** One XML document per filing. Each reporting period is stored in its own directory.

```
data/house/
  2022_Registrations_XML/
    *.xml
  2022_1stQuarter_XML/ ... 2022_4thQuarter_XML/
  2023_Registrations_XML/ ... 2023_4thQuarter_XML/
  2024_Registrations_XML/ ... 2024_4thQuarter_XML/
  2025_Registrations_XML/ ... 2025_4thQuarter_XML/
  2026_Registrations_XML/
  2026_1stQuarter_XML/
```

Root element is `<LOBBYINGDISCLOSURE1>` (registrations) or `<LOBBYINGDISCLOSURE2>` (quarterlies). Filenames are numeric House filing IDs (e.g. `301642857.xml`).

**Key fields.** `organizationName`, `clientName`, `senateID`, `houseID`, `lobbyists/lobbyist[]` (with `coveredPosition`, `lobbyistNew`), `alis/ali_Code[]` (ALI issue codes — up to 9 slots, often sparse), `specific_issues/description` (free text, often naming specific bills and agencies), `income`/`expenses`, `governmentEntities`, `foreignEntities`.

**Source.** [House Clerk Lobbying Disclosure](https://disclosurespreview.house.gov/).

**Timeframe.** 2022-01-01 through 2026-03-31 (2026 = Q1 + registrations).

**Load it.**

```python
from pathlib import Path
import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET

path = Path("data/house/2025_1stQuarter_XML")
for xml_path in sorted(path.glob("*.xml"))[:5]:
    root = ET.parse(xml_path).getroot()
    org = root.findtext("organizationName", "").strip()
    client = root.findtext("clientName", "").strip()
    issues = [a.text for a in root.findall(".//alis/ali_Code") if (a.text or "").strip()]
    print(xml_path.name, "|", org, "→", client, "|", issues)
```

Expect a fair amount of whitespace-only text inside elements; `.strip()` everything. Many quarterly filings carry the same `senateID` as a Senate filing — that's the bridge between the two lobbying datasets.

**Starting points.**
- Senate↔House reconciliation: fuzzy-match on `organizationName`/`clientName` and validate with the shared `senateID`/`houseID`. Discrepancies between the two filings for the same engagement are newsworthy.
- Bill-level lobbying maps: `specific_issues/description` often names bill numbers (`H.R. 1234`) — extract and join against Congress.gov bill and vote data.
- Entity-resolution challenge: the House data has no UUIDs and no standardized casing; building a clean entity table across Senate + House is itself a deliverable.

---

## Cross-dataset leads

The highest-value questions require more than one of these:

- **"Say vs. pay":** For a member (keyed by `bioguide_id` in the press corpus), correlate press-release topics with lobbying activity targeting their chamber/committee around the same quarter.
- **Temporal lobbying–messaging coupling:** When quarterly lobbying spend on an issue spikes, does the language in press releases shift? Good test for causal-adjacent narrative work.
- **Entity graph:** Press-release NER → companies/orgs → Senate/House LDA registrants & clients → government entities lobbied → committees → members. The cross-walks are the investigation.

## Useful external enrichments

- [Congress.gov bulk data / API](https://www.congress.gov/help/using-data-offsite) — bills, votes, committee assignments, cosponsorships. Joins via `bioguide_id`.
- [FEC](https://www.fec.gov/data/) — campaign finance to ground-truth lobbyist contributions.
- [FARA](https://efile.fara.gov/ords/fara/r/fara_ws/api/bulkdata) — foreign-agent filings, complements the `foreign_entities` fields.
- [Federal Register](https://www.federalregister.gov/developers/api/v1) — regulatory outcomes of lobbying on specific rules.

content.text: Now let me look at `sweep_2026.sql` for conventions only, and inspect the actual DB schema.

content.content:
1	-- Lens sweep: the data manual's recommended starting-point questions, run
2	-- against the 2026-Q1 corpus (db/lda_2026.duckdb). Each block is one question.
3	-- Results that become ledger leads cite this file plus the block label.
4	-- Run:  .venv/Scripts/python queries/run_sweep.py db/lda_2026.duckdb
5	
6	-- ==== S1a: biggest spenders by client (Senate, 2026 Q1) ====
7	SELECT client_name, sum(income)::BIGINT AS total_income, count(*) AS filings
8	FROM senate_filings WHERE income IS NOT NULL
9	GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC LIMIT 15;
10	
11	-- ==== S1b: most-active registrants ====
12	SELECT registrant_name, count(*) AS filings,
13	       count(DISTINCT client_name) AS clients, sum(income)::BIGINT AS total_income
14	FROM senate_filings GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY filings DESC LIMIT 15;
15	
16	-- ==== S1c: busiest issue codes ====
17	SELECT a.general_issue_code, count(DISTINCT a.filing_uuid) AS filings,
18	       sum(f.income)::BIGINT AS attributed_income
19	FROM senate_activities a JOIN senate_filings f USING (filing_uuid)
20	GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY filings DESC LIMIT 15;
21	
22	-- ==== S2a: revolving door — ex-congressional staff registering to lobby in 2026 ====
23	-- New registrations (RR) whose lobbyists list covered positions in Congress.
24	SELECT f.registrant_name, f.client_name, l.first_name, l.last_name,
25	       l.covered_position, f.filing_uuid
26	FROM senate_lobbyists l JOIN senate_filings f USING (filing_uuid)
27	WHERE f.filing_type = 'RR'
28	  AND (l.covered_position ILIKE '%senat%' OR l.covered_position ILIKE '%congress%'
29	       OR l.covered_position ILIKE '%rep.%' OR l.covered_position ILIKE '%house%'
30	       OR l.covered_position ILIKE '%chief of staff%')
31	ORDER BY f.registrant_name LIMIT 40;
32	
33	-- ==== S2b: revolving door — most-named offices in covered positions ====
34	SELECT covered_position, count(*) AS n
35	FROM v_covered_positions GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY n DESC LIMIT 20;
36	
37	-- ==== S3: foreign influence — foreign entities behind 2026 lobbying ====
38	SELECT fe.country, fe.name AS foreign_entity, f.client_name, f.registrant_name,
39	       f.filing_uuid
40	FROM senate_foreign_entities fe JOIN senate_filings f USING (filing_uuid)
41	ORDER BY fe.country LIMIT 40;
42	
43	-- ==== S4: contribution flows — honorees in what exists of 2026 LD-203s ====
44	SELECT honoree, count(*) AS items, sum(amount)::BIGINT AS total,
45	       string_agg(DISTINCT contributor_name, '; ') AS contributors
46	FROM senate_contribution_items WHERE honoree IS NOT NULL
47	GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY total DESC NULLS LAST LIMIT 20;
48	
49	-- ==== S5: data-quality — registrants with systematic income gaps ====
50	SELECT registrant_name, count(*) AS filings,
51	       sum(CASE WHEN income IS NULL AND expenses IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS no_money_disclosed
52	FROM senate_filings
53	WHERE filing_type <> 'RR'
54	GROUP BY 1 HAVING count(*) >= 5
55	ORDER BY no_money_disclosed * 1.0 / filings DESC, filings DESC LIMIT 15;
56	
57	-- ==== H1: Senate<->House reconciliation — same org+client, different income (Q1 2026) ====
58	WITH s AS (
59	  SELECT upper(trim(registrant_name)) AS org, upper(trim(client_name)) AS client,
60	         sum(income) AS s_income
61	  FROM senate_filings
62	  WHERE filing_year = 2026 AND filing_type LIKE 'Q1%' GROUP BY 1, 2),
63	h AS (
64	  SELECT upper(trim(organization_name)) AS org, upper(trim(client_name)) AS client,
65	         sum(income) AS h_income
66	  FROM house_filings WHERE report_period = 'Q1' GROUP BY 1, 2)
67	SELECT s.org, s.client, s.s_income::BIGINT AS senate_income,
68	       h.h_income::BIGINT AS house_income,
69	       (s.s_income - h.h_income)::BIGINT AS delta
70	FROM s JOIN h USING (org, client)
71	WHERE s.s_income IS NOT NULL AND h.h_income IS NOT NULL
72	  AND abs(coalesce(s.s_income, 0) - coalesce(h.h_income, 0)) > 5000
73	ORDER BY abs(s.s_income - h.h_income) DESC LIMIT 20;
74	
75	-- ==== H2 / C1: bills lobbied AND pressed — the say-vs-pay bridge (Q1 2026) ====
76	WITH lobbied AS (
77	  SELECT bill, count(*) AS lobby_mentions FROM bill_mentions
78	  WHERE dataset IN ('senate', 'house') GROUP BY 1),
79	pressed AS (
80	  SELECT bill, count(*) AS press_mentions FROM bill_mentions
81	  WHERE dataset = 'press' GROUP BY 1)
82	SELECT bill, lobby_mentions, press_mentions
83	FROM lobbied JOIN pressed USING (bill)
84	ORDER BY lobby_mentions * press_mentions DESC LIMIT 20;
85	
86	-- ==== P3: member press-activity outliers (releases/month vs corpus norm) ====
87	SELECT bioguide_id, name, month, n_releases
88	FROM v_releases_by_member_month
89	WHERE n_releases > 40 ORDER BY n_releases DESC LIMIT 15;
90	
91	-- ==== H1b: reconciliation with amendment/duplicate dedup (supersedes H1) ====
92	-- L001 artifact check (2026-07-04) proved raw sums double-count: registrants file
93	-- duplicates (S-3 Group filed identical Senate Q1s 22s apart) and amendments
94	-- (Senate filing_type '1A'; House refilings under new filing_ids). Keep only the
95	-- latest filing per org+client+period on BOTH sides.
96	WITH s AS (
97	  SELECT upper(trim(registrant_name)) AS org, upper(trim(client_name)) AS client, income
98	  FROM senate_filings
99	  WHERE filing_year = 2026 AND (filing_type LIKE 'Q1%' OR filing_type = '1A')
100	  QUALIFY row_number() OVER (PARTITION BY upper(trim(registrant_name)),
101	    upper(trim(client_name)), filing_period ORDER BY posted DESC) = 1),
102	h AS (
103	  SELECT upper(trim(organization_name)) AS org, upper(trim(client_name)) AS client, income
104	  FROM house_filings WHERE report_period = 'Q1'
105	  QUALIFY row_number() OVER (PARTITION BY upper(trim(organization_name)),
106	    upper(trim(client_name)), report_period ORDER BY CAST(filing_id AS BIGINT) DESC) = 1)
107	SELECT s.org, s.client, s.income::BIGINT AS senate_income, h.income::BIGINT AS house_income,
108	       (s.income - h.income)::BIGINT AS delta
109	FROM s JOIN h USING (org, client)
110	WHERE s.income IS NOT NULL AND h.income IS NOT NULL AND abs(s.income - h.income) > 5000
111	ORDER BY abs(s.income - h.income) DESC LIMIT 20;
112	
113	-- ==== H1c: reconciliation joined on registrant+client IDs (supersedes H1b's name join) ====
114	-- FIXED 2026-07-06 (was a real bug, caught by user cross-checking the live House
115	-- portal): the original version filtered senate to filing_type LIKE 'Q%', which
116	-- SILENTLY EXCLUDES amendment codes (1A/2A/3A/4A/...). House-side dedup (latest
117	-- filing_id within the SAME directory-period bucket, since house doesn't split
118	-- amendments into a separate period) correctly picked up amendments; senate-side
119	-- didn't, so the join compared senate's PRE-amendment figure against house's
120	-- POST-amendment figure. All 4 manually-verified "10x mismatches" from the buggy
121	-- version turned out to be false positives this way (e.g. Mercury/PRVO: senate Q2
122	-- $1.2M + senate 2A $120K on 2025-07-23 == house's $120K exactly; the buggy query
123	-- silently dropped the 2A row and compared $1.2M vs $120K instead). Fix: key
124	-- dedup on filing_period (constant across original+amendment, e.g. "second_quarter"
125	-- for both Q2 and 2A), not filing_type, and don't filter out amendment types --
126	-- only exclude registrations (RR/RA), which aren't quarter reports.
127	WITH s AS (
128	  SELECT registrant_id, client_id, filing_year,
129	         CASE filing_period WHEN 'first_quarter' THEN 'Q1' WHEN 'second_quarter' THEN 'Q2'
130	              WHEN 'third_quarter' THEN 'Q3' WHEN 'fourth_quarter' THEN 'Q4' END AS q,
131	         income, registrant_name, client_name, filing_uuid, filing_type
132	  FROM senate_filings
133	  WHERE filing_type NOT IN ('RR', 'RA')
134	    AND filing_period IN ('first_quarter','second_quarter','third_quarter','fourth_quarter')
135	  QUALIFY row_number() OVER (PARTITION BY registrant_id, client_id, filing_year,
136	    filing_period ORDER BY posted DESC) = 1),
137	h AS (
138	  SELECT split_part(senate_reg_id, '-', 1) AS registrant_id,
139	         split_part(senate_reg_id, '-', 2) AS client_id,
140	         report_year AS filing_year, report_period AS q, income, filing_id
141	  FROM house_filings
142	  WHERE senate_reg_id LIKE '%-%' AND report_period LIKE 'Q%'
143	  QUALIFY row_number() OVER (PARTITION BY senate_reg_id, report_year, report_period
144	    ORDER BY CAST(filing_id AS BIGINT) DESC) = 1)
145	SELECT s.registrant_name, s.client_name, s.filing_year, s.q, s.filing_type AS senate_latest_type,
146	       s.income::BIGINT AS senate_income, h.income::BIGINT AS house_income,
147	       (s.income - h.income)::BIGINT AS delta, s.filing_uuid, h.filing_id
148	FROM s JOIN h USING (registrant_id, client_id, filing_year, q)
149	WHERE s.income IS NOT NULL AND h.income IS NOT NULL
150	  AND abs(s.income - h.income) > 5000
151	ORDER BY abs(s.income - h.income) DESC LIMIT 25;
152	
153	-- ==== H1d: chronic cross-chamber mis-reporters (L001 revisit; needs H1c's ID join) ====
154	-- Aggregates H1c's engagement-quarter deltas by registrant. exact_10x counts
155	-- quarters where one side is exactly 10x the other (missing/extra-zero data entry).
156	-- Uses the SAME fixed dedup as H1c (filing_period-keyed, amendments included).
157	WITH s AS (
158	  SELECT registrant_id, client_id, filing_year,
159	         CASE filing_period WHEN 'first_quarter' THEN 'Q1' WHEN 'second_quarter' THEN 'Q2'
160	              WHEN 'third_quarter' THEN 'Q3' WHEN 'fourth_quarter' THEN 'Q4' END AS q,
161	         income, registrant_name
162	  FROM senate_filings
163	  WHERE filing_type NOT IN ('RR', 'RA')
164	    AND filing_period IN ('first_quarter','second_quarter','third_quarter','fourth_quarter')
165	  QUALIFY row_number() OVER (PARTITION BY registrant_id, client_id, filing_year,
166	    filing_period ORDER BY posted DESC) = 1),
167	h AS (
168	  SELECT split_part(senate_reg_id, '-', 1) AS registrant_id,
169	         split_part(senate_reg_id, '-', 2) AS client_id,
170	         report_year AS filing_year, report_period AS q, income
171	  FROM house_filings
172	  WHERE senate_reg_id LIKE '%-%' AND report_period LIKE 'Q%'
173	  QUALIFY row_number() OVER (PARTITION BY senate_reg_id, report_year, report_period
174	    ORDER BY CAST(filing_id AS BIGINT) DESC) = 1),
175	j AS (
176	  SELECT s.registrant_name, s.income AS si, h.income AS hi
177	  FROM s JOIN h USING (registrant_id, client_id, filing_year, q)
178	  WHERE s.income IS NOT NULL AND h.income IS NOT NULL)
179	SELECT registrant_name,
180	       count(*) AS matched_qtrs,
181	       sum(CASE WHEN abs(si - hi) > 5000 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS mismatched,
182	       sum(CASE WHEN si = 10 * hi OR hi = 10 * si THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS exact_10x,
183	       sum(abs(si - hi))::BIGINT AS total_abs_delta
184	FROM j GROUP BY 1
185	HAVING sum(CASE WHEN abs(si - hi) > 5000 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) >= 3
186	ORDER BY mismatched DESC, total_abs_delta DESC LIMIT 20;
187	
188	-- ==== S1d: top spenders by RESOLVED client entity (needs entity tables) ====
189	-- Raw S1a fragments clients across name variants; this groups by norm_key.
190	SELECT e.canonical_name, sum(f.income)::BIGINT AS total_income,
191	       count(*) AS filings, count(DISTINCT f.client_name) AS name_variants
192	FROM senate_filings f
193	JOIN entity_aliases ea ON ea.kind = 'client' AND ea.dataset = 'senate'
194	                       AND ea.raw_name = f.client_name
195	JOIN entities e ON e.entity_id = ea.entity_id
196	WHERE f.income IS NOT NULL
197	GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC LIMIT 15;
198	
199	-- ==== C1b: say-vs-pay weighted by dollars (supersedes mention counts) ====
200	-- Caveat: income is filing-level; a filing naming several bills attributes its
201	-- full income to each. Use for ranking, not exact dollars.
202	WITH m AS (SELECT DISTINCT bill, dataset, record_key FROM bill_mentions
203	           WHERE dataset IN ('senate', 'house')),
204	lob AS (
205	  SELECT m.bill, count(*) AS n_filings,
206	         sum(coalesce(sf.income, hf.income, 0))::BIGINT AS attributed_dollars
207	  FROM m LEFT JOIN senate_filings sf ON m.dataset = 'senate' AND m.record_key = sf.filing_uuid
208	         LEFT JOIN house_filings hf ON m.dataset = 'house' AND m.record_key = hf.filing_id
209	  GROUP BY 1),
210	p AS (SELECT bill, count(*) AS press_mentions FROM bill_mentions
211	      WHERE dataset = 'press' GROUP BY 1)
212	SELECT lob.bill, n_filings, attributed_dollars, coalesce(press_mentions, 0) AS press_mentions
213	FROM lob LEFT JOIN p ON lob.bill = p.bill
214	ORDER BY attributed_dollars DESC LIMIT 25;
